Neural networks can be equipped with prior known symmetries to enhance data efficiency. Recent works have shown that hard coding symmetries into a neural network may not always lead to better generalization, indicating the importance of learned equivariance.

Symmetries learned by neural networks reflect true symmetries in the data. The capability of neural networks for learning symmetries can be supported by studying how accurately learned symmetries reflect true data symmetries.

Various methods have been proposed to train more robust neural networks. Features extracted by a model pretrained on a bigger dataset often demonstrate more robust results. There are many effective techniques for training more robust feature extractors.
Our method allows for better understanding and explaining what training regimes lead to more invariant, and thus more robust representations.
A symmetry is a transformation of an object that leaves the object unchanged. The set of all such transformations of an object with composition as a binary operation forms a group.
The theory of Lie groups is a sweet spot of mathematics that helps to formalize symmetries and provides practical methods for studying them.
A Lie group is formally defined as a group with the structure of a differential manifold.
The tangent space in the identity element of a Lie group forms a vector space called Lie algebra.
A Lie algebra determines a Lie group up to an isomorphism for simply connected Lie groups, making it a more convenient object to study.
To recover a simply connected symmetry group, one needs to understand its Lie algebra.

Networks with more parameters tend to learn to a higher degree of invariance, resulting in consistently smaller symmetry variance. Networks with fewer parameters learn less precise symmetries, leading to higher symmetry bias.

Networks with more parameters tend to achieve higher invariance compared to their shallow counterparts. Sufficiently parameterized deep networks are more capable of learning invariances than wide networks with the same number of parameters.
The number of parameters in a network influences the learned symmetrical properties. Networks with more parameters tend to exhibit smaller symmetry variance and more precise symmetries.

Sufficiently parameterized deep networks are more capable of learning invariances compared to wide networks with the same number of parameters. Deeper networks become more invariant in the direction of learned symmetries.
Networks with a balanced width and depth achieve the smallest symmetry bias. The depth of a network impacts its capability to learn invariances and symmetries.
Symmetry variance and symmetry bias have a moderate negative correlation with classification accuracy. More accurate models tend to have lower symmetry variance and lower symmetry bias.
Neural networks in this context have the capability to learn symmetries differently, showcasing how the networks are flexible in handling various symmetries during testing.

The symmetry learned by the neural network depends on a network's configuration and training regime. It is important to improve the interpretability of symmetry learning to identify configurations and training regimes that facilitate training invariances in neural networks.

The symmetry learned by the neural network depends on a network's configuration and training regime. It is important to design efficient ways of incorporating symmetries into a model by improving the interpretability of symmetry learning.

Improving the interpretability of symmetry learning can facilitate the design of efficient ways of incorporating symmetries into a model and identify configurations and training regimes that facilitate training invariances in neural networks.

LieGG is currently limited to instances of GL(n) connected group, which limits the analysis of complex invariances. This limitation hinders the analysis where a transformation cannot be described as a group action.

Extending a mechanism of retrieving symmetries to more complex transformations is an intriguing direction for future research. It can provide a more general understanding of symmetry learning in neural networks.

In our analysis, LieGG is a building block to increasing the interpretability of symmetry learning in neural networks. Further large-scale analysis of LieGG in complex scenarios and with more advanced models can provide a more general understanding of symmetry learning.

Our analysis focuses on the specific family of models, i.e. feed-forward multi-layer perceptions trained on datasets with well-controlled factors of variations. Large-scale analysis of LieGG in complex scenarios can provide a more general understanding of symmetry learning in neural networks.
Increasing the interpretability of symmetry learning in neural networks is crucial. It can facilitate training invariances and provide a better understanding of symmetry learning.
